lines.hr: Vous êtes sortis en ville ?
lines.en: Did you go out into town?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Oui, après le déjeuner, nous sommes allés au marché.
lines.en: Yes, after lunch, we went to the market.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Et le soir ?
lines.en: And in the evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Avant de me coucher, j'ai regardé un vieux film.
lines.en: Before going to bed, I watched an old film.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Et dimanche, tu es resté à la maison ?
lines.en: And on Sunday, did you stay at home?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non, on a fini la journée par une longue promenade.
lines.en: No, we ended the day with a long walk.

days.title: Décrire un lieu : la ville, le quartier, la campagne
days.objective: You can describe a neighbourhood or a village and locate places in relation to each other.
days.paretoFocus: The relative word 'où' links a place to what happens there, which lets you replace two short sentences with one natural description.
drills: Write three sentences about your area, each containing où.
Locate four places near you using compass points or position phrases.
Describe the countryside and a city centre in three contrasting sentences.

items: Which relative word introduces a place, and how is it different from que?
Which verb means 'to be located'?
How do you say a village is surrounded by fields?

activities.title: Le quartier où j'habite
activities.intro: The relative word 'où' links a noun of place to a whole clause, and unlike qui or que it never changes form. 'Se trouver' is the standard verb for saying where something is located. Position is given with compass points or with fixed phrases like en bas de and au bout de.

items.hr: J'habite dans un quartier où il y a beaucoup de petits commerces.
items.en: I live in a neighbourhood where there are a lot of small shops.
items.note: où refers to a place, never to a person

items.hr: La gare se trouve au nord de la ville.
items.en: The station is located in the north of the town.
items.note: se trouver = to be located

items.hr: C'est un village calme, entouré de champs et de forêts.
items.en: It is a quiet village, surrounded by fields and forests.
items.note: entouré de + nom, no article after de here

items.hr: En bas de la rue, il y a une place avec une fontaine.
items.en: At the bottom of the street, there is a square with a fountain.
items.note: en bas de, au bout de, en face de

items.hr: À la campagne, tout est plus loin, mais l'air est meilleur.
items.en: In the countryside, everything is further away, but the air is better.
items.note: à la campagne, en ville, en banlieue

activities.title: Situer et décrire
activities.intro: Practise the place relative and the vocabulary of describing an area.

questions.prompt: C'est un quartier ___ il y a beaucoup de commerces.
questions.explanation: The clause says what happens in that place, so the place relative où is needed, not an object or subject relative.

questions.prompt: La gare se ___ au nord de la ville.
questions.explanation: The reflexive verb is conjugated in the present with the singular subject la gare.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Le village est ___ de champs.' (has fields all around it)
questions.explanation: Entouré de plus a plain noun says what surrounds the place, and it agrees with the masculine village.

questions.prompt: Complete the position phrase: 'En ___ de la rue, il y a une place.' (at the lower end)
questions.explanation: En bas de is the fixed opposite of en haut de and always keeps de before the noun.

questions.prompt: Build the French for: 'In the countryside, everything is further away.'
questions.explanation: À la campagne is fixed, and plus loin is the comparative of the adverb loin.

activities.title: Tu habites où exactement ?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A new colleague asks about your area, so describe it using où, se trouver and position phrases.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu habites dans quel quartier ?
lines.en: Which neighbourhood do you live in?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dans un quartier tranquille où il y a beaucoup de petits commerces.
lines.en: In a quiet neighbourhood where there are a lot of small shops.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: C'est loin de la gare ?
lines.en: Is it far from the station?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non, la gare se trouve au nord, à dix minutes à pied.
lines.en: No, the station is located to the north, ten minutes on foot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Il y a des endroits agréables pour boire un café ?
lines.en: Are there nice places to have a coffee?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Oui, en bas de ma rue, il y a une place avec une fontaine.
lines.en: Yes, at the bottom of my street, there is a square with a fountain.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Moi, je viens d'un petit village.
lines.en: I come from a small village.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: À la campagne, tout est plus loin, mais l'air est meilleur.
lines.en: In the countryside, everything is further away, but the air is better.

days.title: Exprimer ses goûts et ses préférences
days.objective: You can say what appeals to you and highlight what you like using ce qui and ce que.
days.paretoFocus: 'Plaire' reverses the English word order and 'ce qui / ce que' is the standard way to emphasise a taste, so together they lift your speech above aimer alone.
drills: Write three sentences with plaire, changing the person each time.
Write two highlighting sentences that start with ce qui or ce que.
Say three things you prefer, comparing them to something else.

items: In a plaire sentence, who is the subject: the person or the thing?
When do you use ce qui rather than ce que?
Which preposition follows préférer when you compare two things?

activities.title: Ce qui me plaît
activities.intro: With plaire, the thing you like is the subject and the person is an indirect pronoun, so 'ce film me plaît' literally means the film is pleasing to me. To highlight a taste, start with ce qui when the following verb needs a subject, and ce que when it needs an object, then continue with c'est. Préférer compares two things with à.

items.hr: Ce film m'a beaucoup plu.
items.en: I liked that film a lot.
items.note: the participle of plaire is plu and stays invariable

items.hr: Ce que j'aime, c'est marcher le matin.
items.en: What I like is walking in the morning.
items.note: ce que + subject + verb, then c'est

items.hr: Ce qui me plaît ici, c'est le calme.
items.en: What I like here is the quiet.
items.note: ce qui is followed directly by the verb

items.hr: La musique classique ne me plaît pas du tout.
items.en: I do not like classical music at all.
items.note: the negation wraps around plaît, with the pronoun inside

items.hr: Je préfère les séries aux films, elles durent plus longtemps.
items.en: I prefer series to films, they last longer.
items.note: préférer A à B, with à plus les giving aux

activities.title: Goûts et préférences
activities.intro: Practise plaire and the two highlighting words.

questions.prompt: ___ me plaît ici, c'est le calme.
questions.explanation: The verb plaît has no other subject, so the subject form ce qui is required.

questions.prompt: Ce film m'a beaucoup ___.
questions.explanation: After the auxiliary you need the past participle of plaire, which is plu.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Je préfère les séries ___ films.' (to the)
questions.explanation: Préférer compares with à, and à plus les contracts to aux.

questions.prompt: Complete with the present tense: 'La musique classique ne me ___ pas du tout.' (appeal to me)
questions.explanation: The subject is la musique classique, so the verb is third person singular with a circumflex on the i.

questions.prompt: Build the French for: 'What I like is walking in the morning.'
questions.explanation: Ce que introduces the object clause, and the second half always starts with c'est.

activities.title: Chacun ses goûts
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend compares tastes with you, so reach for plaire and the two highlighting structures you just learned.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu as vu le film hier soir ? Il t'a plu ?
lines.en: Did you see the film last night? Did you like it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Oui, ce film m'a beaucoup plu, surtout la fin.
lines.en: Yes, I liked that film a lot, especially the ending.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Moi, je préfère les séries.
lines.en: I prefer series.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Je préfère les séries aux films aussi, elles durent plus longtemps.
lines.en: I prefer series to films too, they last longer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Et la musique classique, ça te dit quelque chose ?
lines.en: And classical music, does that appeal to you at all?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Franchement, la musique classique ne me plaît pas du tout.
lines.en: Honestly, I do not like classical music at all.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Qu'est-ce que tu aimes, alors ?
lines.en: What do you like, then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ce que j'aime, c'est écouter un concert en marchant le matin.
lines.en: What I like is listening to a concert while walking in the morning.

days.title: Comparer deux villes, deux produits, deux habitudes
days.objective: You can compare quantities and say that two things are the same, different or similar.
days.paretoFocus: Comparing amounts needs 'autant de' and 'moins de' with de before the noun, a detail that learners drop constantly and that changes the meaning.
drills: Write three sentences comparing quantities of noise, traffic or work in two places.
Write two sentences with le même que and two with différent de.
Compare two products you own in four sentences.

items: Which small word must come between autant and the noun?
Which preposition follows différent?
How does même agree, and what follows it in a comparison?

activities.title: Le même ou pas le même
activities.intro: When you compare amounts of a noun, French inserts de: autant de bruit, moins de circulation, plus de travail. Sameness uses le même, la même or les mêmes plus que, while difference uses différent de. Se ressembler says two things look or feel alike without naming a criterion.

items.hr: Ce téléphone a les mêmes fonctions que l'autre, mais il coûte moins cher.
items.en: This phone has the same functions as the other one, but it costs less.
items.note: le même / la même / les mêmes + que

items.hr: Il y a autant de bruit ici qu'au centre-ville.
items.en: There is as much noise here as in the town centre.
items.note: autant de + nom + que, and que elides to qu'

items.hr: Ma vie à la campagne est très différente de ma vie en ville.
items.en: My life in the countryside is very different from my life in town.
items.note: différent de, agreeing with the subject

items.hr: Dans ce quartier, il y a moins de circulation que dans le mien.
items.en: In this neighbourhood there is less traffic than in mine.
items.note: moins de + nom, never moins que before a noun

items.hr: Les deux villes se ressemblent beaucoup.
items.en: The two towns are very similar.
items.note: se ressembler is reflexive and plural here

activities.title: Deux choses, une comparaison
activities.intro: Practise comparing quantities and expressing sameness or difference.

questions.prompt: Ce téléphone a les ___ fonctions que l'autre.
questions.explanation: Même is an adjective here and agrees with the plural noun fonctions.

questions.prompt: Il y a autant ___ bruit ici qu'au centre-ville.
questions.explanation: Quantity words are followed by plain de before the noun, with no article.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ma vie ici est très différente ___ ma vie d'avant.' (from)
questions.explanation: Différent is always followed by de, not by que.

questions.prompt: Complete with the present tense: 'Les deux villes se ___ beaucoup.' (look alike)
questions.explanation: The subject is plural, so the reflexive verb takes the ent ending.

questions.prompt: Build the French for: 'There is less traffic in this neighbourhood.'
questions.explanation: Moins is followed by de plus the bare noun, and the place phrase comes at the end.

activities.title: Deux villes, deux vies
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend who moved compares her old and new life, so answer with quantity comparisons and sameness words.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Alors, ta nouvelle ville, c'est comment ?
lines.en: So, what is your new town like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ma vie ici est très différente de ma vie d'avant.
lines.en: My life here is very different from my life before.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: C'est plus calme ?
lines.en: Is it quieter?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Pas vraiment, il y a autant de bruit qu'au centre-ville.
lines.en: Not really, there is as much noise as in the town centre.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Et la circulation ?
lines.en: And the traffic?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Là, il y a moins de circulation que dans mon ancien quartier.
lines.en: There, there is less traffic than in my old neighbourhood.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Au fond, les deux villes se ressemblent un peu, non ?
lines.en: Deep down, the two towns are a bit similar, right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Oui, elles ont les mêmes problèmes, mais ici tout coûte moins cher.
lines.en: Yes, they have the same problems, but here everything costs less.

days.title: Le recyclage et les gestes écologiques simples
days.objective: You can talk about sorting waste and describe habits that are increasing or decreasing.
days.paretoFocus: 'De plus en plus' and 'de moins en moins' express any trend in three words, and they work with nouns, verbs and adjectives alike.
drills: Write two sentences with de plus en plus and two with de moins en moins.
List four things you sort or recycle at home.
Say three small habits you could change this month.

items: What extra word do the trend phrases need before a noun?
Which preposition follows essayer before an infinitive?
How do you say you sort your waste every day?

activities.title: Trier et gaspiller moins
activities.intro: 'De plus en plus' and 'de moins en moins' describe a growing or shrinking trend, and they add de before a noun. 'Essayer de' and 'éviter de' both take an infinitive, while éviter takes a plain noun when there is no verb. 'Penser à' plus an infinitive means to remember to do something.

items.hr: Je trie mes déchets: le verre, le papier et le plastique.
items.en: I sort my waste: glass, paper and plastic.
items.note: trier = to sort, les déchets = waste

items.hr: J'essaie d'éviter les sacs en plastique.
items.en: I try to avoid plastic bags.
items.note: essayer de + infinitif, éviter + nom

items.hr: On gaspille de moins en moins d'eau à la maison.
items.en: We waste less and less water at home.
items.note: de moins en moins de + nom

items.hr: De plus en plus de gens vont au travail à vélo.
items.en: More and more people go to work by bike.
items.note: de plus en plus de + nom, at the start of the sentence

items.hr: Pense à éteindre la lumière en sortant.
items.en: Remember to turn off the light on your way out.
items.note: penser à + infinitif = remember to

activities.title: Les petits gestes
activities.intro: Practise trend phrases and the everyday vocabulary of recycling.

questions.prompt: On gaspille ___ d'eau qu'avant.
questions.explanation: The decreasing trend is a fixed three word phrase, and it keeps de before the noun.

questions.prompt: J'essaie ___ éviter les sacs en plastique.
questions.explanation: Essayer takes de before an infinitive, and de elides before the vowel of éviter.

questions.prompt: Complete with the present tense: 'Je ___ mes déchets tous les jours.' (separate them into bins)
questions.explanation: Trier is a regular er verb, so the je form ends in e.

questions.prompt: Complete with an infinitive: 'Pense à ___ la lumière.' (switch it off)
questions.explanation: Éteindre is the verb for switching off a light, and it stays in the infinitive after penser à.

questions.prompt: Build the French for: 'More and more people go to work by bike.'
questions.explanation: The trend phrase needs de before gens, and transport uses à vélo with no article.

activities.title: On trie tout ça ?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A flatmate asks about recycling at home, so use trend phrases and the verbs of this lesson.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Je mets le carton dans quelle poubelle ?
lines.en: Which bin do I put the cardboard in?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dans la jaune. Je trie mes déchets: le verre, le papier et le plastique.
lines.en: In the yellow one. I sort my waste: glass, paper and plastic.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu prends encore des sacs au supermarché ?
lines.en: Do you still take bags at the supermarket?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non, j'essaie d'éviter les sacs en plastique.
lines.en: No, I try to avoid plastic bags.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Et l'eau ? On en utilise beaucoup ici.
lines.en: And water? We use a lot of it here.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: On gaspille de moins en moins d'eau depuis l'année dernière.
lines.en: We have been wasting less and less water since last year.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Je devrais aussi venir au bureau autrement.
lines.en: I should also come to the office differently.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: De plus en plus de gens vont au travail à vélo, essaie une semaine.
lines.en: More and more people go to work by bike, try it for a week.

days.title: Donner un conseil et réagir au problème de quelqu'un
days.objective: You can give advice with conditional forms and react kindly when someone tells you about a problem.
days.paretoFocus: 'Tu devrais' and 'à ta place, je' are the two advice formulas French speakers actually use, and they soften what would otherwise sound like an order.
drills: Write three pieces of advice with tu devrais for three different problems.
Write two sentences starting with à ta place.
Say three short reassuring reactions to a friend's bad news.

items: What are the conditional forms of devoir and pouvoir for tu?
Which verb form follows 'à ta place'?
Give two ways to reassure someone in one short sentence.

activities.title: Tu devrais te reposer
activities.intro: Advice uses the conditional, built on the future stem plus the imperfect endings, so devoir gives tu devrais and pouvoir gives tu pourrais. 'À ta place' sets up a hypothetical and is followed by a conditional verb. 'Ce serait mieux de' plus an infinitive suggests a better option without naming anyone.

items.hr: Tu devrais te reposer un peu.
items.en: You should rest a bit.
items.note: devoir in the conditional: je devrais, tu devrais

items.hr: À ta place, je prendrais rendez-vous tout de suite.
items.en: If I were you, I would make an appointment right away.
items.note: à ta place + conditionnel

items.hr: Tu pourrais lui envoyer un message pour expliquer.
items.en: You could send him a message to explain.
items.note: pouvoir in the conditional: tu pourrais

items.hr: Ce serait mieux d'en parler calmement.
items.en: It would be better to talk about it calmly.
items.note: être in the conditional: ce serait

items.hr: Ne t'inquiète pas, ça va s'arranger.
items.en: Do not worry, it will work out.
items.note: s'arranger = to sort itself out

activities.title: Un bon conseil
activities.intro: Practise conditional advice forms and reassuring reactions.

questions.prompt: Tu ___ te reposer un peu, tu as l'air fatigué.
questions.explanation: Advice needs the conditional, formed on the future stem devr with the imperfect ending ais.

questions.prompt: À ta place, je ___ rendez-vous tout de suite.
questions.explanation: After à ta place the situation is imagined, so the verb goes into the conditional.

questions.prompt: Complete with the conditional of être: 'Ce ___ mieux d'en parler calmement.'
questions.explanation: Être has the irregular stem ser, giving ce serait in the conditional.

questions.prompt: Complete with the conditional of pouvoir, tu form: 'Tu ___ lui envoyer un message.'
questions.explanation: Pouvoir has the stem pourr, and the tu ending in the conditional is ais.

questions.prompt: Build the French for: 'Do not worry, it will work out.'
questions.explanation: In a negative command the pronoun stays before the verb, inside ne and pas.

activities.title: Qu'est-ce que je fais ?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend describes a problem at work, so react and advise with conditional forms.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Je dors mal depuis deux semaines, je suis épuisée.
lines.en: I have been sleeping badly for two weeks, I am exhausted.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tu devrais te reposer un peu, tu travailles trop.
lines.en: You should rest a bit, you work too much.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Je sais, mais j'ai un projet à finir.
lines.en: I know, but I have a project to finish.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: À ta place, je prendrais rendez-vous chez le médecin tout de suite.
lines.en: If I were you, I would make a doctor's appointment right away.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Et pour mon chef ? Il ne comprendra pas.
lines.en: And what about my boss? He will not understand.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tu pourrais lui envoyer un message pour expliquer la situation.
lines.en: You could send him a message to explain the situation.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu crois vraiment ?
lines.en: Do you really think so?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Oui, ce serait mieux d'en parler calmement. Ne t'inquiète pas, ça va s'arranger.
lines.en: Yes, it would be better to talk about it calmly. Do not worry, it will work out.

days.title: Réviser l'essentiel : les pièges les plus fréquents
days.objective: You can avoid the five confusions that most often break otherwise correct French sentences.
days.paretoFocus: These pairs appear in almost every conversation, so fixing them once removes a large share of the mistakes a listener actually notices.
drills: Write two sentences contrasting the two verbs meaning 'to know'.
Write three sentences, one with each of the three duration words.
Write two sentences that show the difference between carrying an object and accompanying a person.

items: Which verb of knowing goes with a person or a place?
Which duration word is used for a period that is finished?
Which word is an adjective and which is an adverb in the good and well pair?

activities.title: Les pièges les plus fréquents
activities.intro: Connaître goes with people, places and things you are familiar with, while savoir goes with facts and skills. Depuis covers a period still going on, pendant a finished period, and il y a a point in the past. Bon is an adjective, bien is an adverb, and apporter is for objects while amener is for people.

items.hr: Je connais ce quartier, mais je ne sais pas où est la poste.
items.en: I know this neighbourhood, but I do not know where the post office is.
items.note: connaître + nom, savoir + information

items.hr: J'habite ici depuis deux ans, avant j'étais à Lille.
items.en: I have lived here for two years, before that I was in Lille.
items.note: depuis = the situation is still true

items.hr: J'ai travaillé pendant trois heures, puis j'ai arrêté.
items.en: I worked for three hours, then I stopped.
items.note: pendant = a finished period

items.hr: Ce restaurant est bon et on y mange bien.
items.en: This restaurant is good and you eat well there.
items.note: bon describes a noun, bien describes a verb

items.hr: J'apporte le dessert et j'amène mon frère.
items.en: I am bringing the dessert and bringing my brother along.
items.note: apporter for objects, amener for people

activities.title: Ne plus se tromper
activities.intro: Practise choosing correctly between the pairs that cause the most mistakes.

questions.prompt: Je ___ ce quartier depuis longtemps.
questions.explanation: Familiarity with a place takes connaître; savoir would need a fact or a clause after it.

questions.prompt: J'ai travaillé ___ trois heures hier, puis j'ai arrêté.
questions.explanation: The work is over, so the finished period word is needed; depuis would mean it still continues.

questions.prompt: Complete with the adjective: 'Ce gâteau est très ___.' (tasty)
questions.explanation: The word describes the noun gâteau, so the adjective form is required, not the adverb.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'J'___ mon frère à la fête ce soir.' (bring a person with you, present tense)
questions.explanation: People are accompanied with amener, and the je form has a grave accent on the first e of the ending.

questions.prompt: Build the French for: 'I have lived here for two years.'
questions.explanation: French keeps the present tense with depuis when the situation still continues today.

activities.title: Tu es sûr de ton français ?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend corrects your French while you plan an evening, so use each tricky pair correctly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu connais un bon restaurant dans le coin ?
lines.en: Do you know a good restaurant around here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Je connais ce quartier, mais je ne sais pas si le nouveau est ouvert.
lines.en: I know this neighbourhood, but I do not know if the new one is open.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu habites ici depuis longtemps, non ?
lines.en: You have lived here a long time, right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: J'habite ici depuis deux ans, avant j'étais à Lille.
lines.en: I have lived here for two years, before that I was in Lille.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tu as l'air fatigué ce soir.
lines.en: You look tired tonight.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: J'ai travaillé pendant trois heures après le bureau.
lines.en: I worked for three hours after the office.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Alors on se retrouve à vingt heures. Tu viens seul ?
lines.en: So we meet at eight. Are you coming alone?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non, j'apporte le dessert et j'amène mon frère, il mange bien partout.
lines.en: No, I am bringing the dessert and bringing my brother, he eats well anywhere.
